title: Why hasnt there been any anime newer than eva
display_title: Why hasnt there been any anime newer than eva
wikitext:
[]: Why hasn't there been any anime newer than eva?             Quote Post by GAP » Tue May 12, 2009 5:54 pm I read the AN article the other dasy on why hasn't there been any anime newer than Evangelion. I don't know if it is true or not but Eva has really inspired a lot of people and it has impacted the industry more than any other anime yet Anno himself says there hasn't been anime newer than eva. Why hasn't there been? Isn't there any anime that has at least reached the level of Eva? I guess not otherwise I would hear about it all the time as much as here about other "classic" anime such as Sailor Moon, Dragonball Z or Gundam but why hasn't there been any anime newer than Eva?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue May 12, 2009 6:24 pm Before we can debate about the statement with anything resembling a semblance of accuracy or articulation, presenting the original source would be awfully nice. I could go Google something up, but you're OP. Hop to it!

[]:             Quote Post by Axell » Tue May 12, 2009 7:09 pm newer?

[]:             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Tue May 12, 2009 8:40 pm Axell wrote: newer?

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Tue May 12, 2009 9:54 pm It's true. After Evangelion began in 1995, every single studio out there stopped making anime and they've never been able to start back up.

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Tue May 12, 2009 9:59 pm here is the link: [url]http://www.animenewsnetwork.com/news/2007-02-20/hideaki-anno-releases-statement-about-new-evangelion-movies[/url] Based on your starting post are you actually just asking "Why hasn't there been an anime on the same level as NGE?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue May 12, 2009 10:06 pm Ah, I remember that poster now. Not much to say here other than that Anno was being a pompous twit.

[]:             Quote Post by Synapsid » Tue May 12, 2009 10:28 pm @NAveryW It might be more accurate to say Gainax hasn't released anything on the same level ever since.

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed May 13, 2009 12:00 am Reichu wrote: Not much to say here other than that Anno was being a pompous twit.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed May 13, 2009 1:43 am Alaska Slim wrote: Sure, the statement was over-generalized, but his concern is that the anime industry is stagnating, which it is, and is perhaps the driving reason why it's been in decline recently with fewer shows being produced, and fewer still becoming successful.

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed May 13, 2009 2:56 am Mr. Tines wrote: Fewer shows? Chartfag suggests not.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed May 13, 2009 4:05 am Alaska Slim wrote: And lest we forget, Evangelion didn't become that popular until around its second run through, when its latter parts had gained notoriety.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Wed May 13, 2009 4:13 am And mecha designs in Eva aren't anything new, look at Bubblegum Crisis 2032's mechs (the one from 1987). Slender, sleek, human proportions, and even shoulder pylons! With their single horn sticking out of their forehead and going berserker all over the place, and I'm not talking about the Knight Saber's power suits. I'm not sure where the claim that Eva changed mecha designs in anime came from, although it's not the first time I've heard it. I don't even think Evangelion is nearly as good as a lot of other shows, some of them being made after 1997.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed May 13, 2009 5:05 am Reichu wrote: Otherwise, I'm going to wordfilter "Mr. Anno" into something obscene.

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Wed May 13, 2009 9:27 am It is safe to assume that anybody on these forums is a fanatic on some level, but as anime fans I think we're all aware that there are plenty of good shows out there. Look at Evangelion as a "seminal" work. Seminal works are rare and influential, and do not sit in a vacuum. Artists constantly cannibalize themselves and those around them. Ideas constantly flow, they do not just manifest from nothing. You just have to keep on ... uh... navigating the river... of... animation? To... yeah... I give up.

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed May 13, 2009 1:53 pm Reichu wrote: Let's see some source on that. (Come on, you know the drill by now.)

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu May 14, 2009 4:28 pm As far as "being on the same level as Evangelion " in terms of having a really complex plot and backstory that you can piece together through years of work and research, I haven't been able to find anything else anime or otherwise. Not just what's come after Evangelion but before it as well. See this thread for discussion on that and feel free to give any examples I've overlooked. Please.

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Thu May 14, 2009 5:57 pm NAveryW wrote: feel free to give any examples I've overlooked. Please.

[]:             Quote Post by iamsynch » Sun May 17, 2009 3:17 am Eva was an accident. The right man was at the right place at the right time in both his life and Japan's social psyche. Revolutions are by its very nature unpredictable and irregular. If they occurred with a static rhythm they would lose their meaning and significance. I agree with Tines about the EoE. Anno managed to execute a perfect (in the esthetic sense) ending, and it was what happened there to make me look back and rewatch the series to see what I missed. Everything before to me was a prelude and buildup, sort of like, . A lot of shows being with a bang and end with a whimper. The two shows I watched this season (Casshern, Michiko no Hatchin) both fit this description. ------ http://www.animenewsnetwork.com/news/2009-05-04/industry-group-head-says-anime-is-a-bubble-that-burst Something in this article caught my eye. >>Yamaguchi concludes that Japan is already falling behind China in the number of titles produced every year. He said that Japan should emphasize quality over quantity, such as teaching anime production in national universities and raising better workers, as the way to strengthen Japan's economy. >> falling behind China in the number of titles What?

[]:             Quote Post by Gendo'sPapa » Sun May 17, 2009 5:36 pm Considering 90% of anime now seems to be built around cute moe-blobs I do gotta say the anime industry holds nothing of interest at the moment. Anno was definitely being extreme when he wrote his comment but I give the man credit for saying it. There's maybe 5 anime directors who are actual filmmakers in the world and I consider Anno to be one of those, the rest are just making product that fans want. And fans are essentially greedy (hence why we want Eva to have a TV series, a manga, two films, a remake animated film series, and a live action remake), I fall weakness to this often.
